cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A global network that connects billions of devices via agreed-upon protocols — built on existing telecommunication infrastructure

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: An agreed-upon set of rules for how data is formatted and transmitted between devices on a network

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A unique numeric address assigned to each device on a network — used to route data to the correct destination

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A network device that forwards data packets between networks, determining the best path for transmission

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection per second

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A small unit of data containing a portion of a message along with source and destination IP addresses — transmitted independently across a network

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A protocol that ensures reliable delivery of data packets across a network — breaks data into packets, sends them, and retransmits any that are lost

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The protocol used to request and deliver web pages between browsers and web servers

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The existence of multiple paths between network nodes so that if one path fails, data can still be delivered via another route

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A property of a system that allows it to continue operating correctly even when some of its components fail

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The collection of web pages and other resources connected by hyperlinks and accessed via the Internet using HTTP

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The ability of a system to handle increasing workload or expanding use without performance degradation

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A system that translates human-readable domain names (like google.com) into numerical IP addresses

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The process of determining the path a packet takes from source to destination across a network

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A group of computing devices connected together to communicate and share resources

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The execution of multiple tasks one at a time in sequence — one process must finish before the next begins

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A computing model where multiple tasks run simultaneously on multiple processors — reduces total execution time for divisible tasks

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A computing model where a task is divided across multiple computers, each handling part of the work — enables solving problems too large for one machine

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The ratio of time to complete a task sequentially divided by the time to complete it in parallel — measures the benefit of parallelization

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A physical device (computer, phone, server) that can run programs and connect to a network

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains how packet switching allows the Internet to be fault tolerant?

choices.text: Packets can take different paths through the network, so if one path fails, packets are rerouted automatically
choices.explanation: Packet switching allows each packet to travel independently via different routes — if a link fails, packets automatically reroute through redundant paths.

choices.text: Packet switching compresses data so it can be stored if a connection fails
choices.explanation: Packet switching routes data, it does not compress or store it — fault tolerance comes from redundant routing paths.

choices.text: Each packet is sent to every device on the network to ensure it arrives
choices.explanation: Broadcasting to all devices would be extremely inefficient — packet switching routes packets only to their destination.

choices.text: The sender waits for confirmation from every router before sending the next packet
choices.explanation: Waiting for confirmation from every router would be slow — TCP handles delivery confirmation at endpoints, not intermediate routers.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A task takes 100 seconds to run on one processor. When run using 4 processors in parallel, it takes 30 seconds. What is the speedup?

choices.text: Approximately 3.3 (100 ÷ 30)
choices.explanation: Speedup = sequential time ÷ parallel time = 100 ÷ 30 ≈ 3.3. The speedup is less than 4 processors because some parts of the task cannot be parallelized.

choices.text: Exactly 4 (equal to the number of processors)
choices.explanation: Perfect linear speedup (equal to processor count) rarely occurs — some tasks have sequential portions that limit parallelization.

choices.text: 70 (the time saved)
choices.explanation: 70 seconds is the time saved, not the speedup ratio — speedup is calculated as sequential time divided by parallel time.
0.3 would indicate the program got slower — speedup is sequential divided by parallel time (100÷30 ≈ 3.3), not the other way around.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A network has only one path connecting two locations. What is the primary concern with this design?

choices.text: If that single path fails, the two locations cannot communicate at all — the system lacks fault tolerance
choices.explanation: Fault tolerance requires redundant paths — with only one path, any failure in that link completely disconnects the two locations.

choices.text: The network will be too fast and cause bandwidth overflow
choices.explanation: A single path may actually limit bandwidth — but the primary concern is that any failure makes the system completely non-functional.

choices.text: Packets will circle endlessly because there is only one direction of travel
choices.explanation: Packet routing does not loop endlessly on a single path — the concern is failure, not circular routing.

choices.text: IP addresses cannot be assigned with only one path
choices.explanation: IP addresses are assigned to devices, not network paths — a single-path network can still assign IP addresses correctly.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Which of the following best describes the difference between the Internet and the World Wide Web?

choices.text: The Internet is the global network infrastructure; the World Wide Web is one application built on top of the Internet
choices.explanation: The Internet is the underlying network of connected computers. The Web is a collection of web pages and resources accessed via HTTP — one of many applications that use the Internet.

choices.text: They are the same thing — Internet and World Wide Web are interchangeable terms
choices.explanation: They are different — the Internet is the hardware and protocol infrastructure; the Web is one application that runs on it alongside email, FTP, and others.

choices.text: The World Wide Web is the hardware; the Internet is the collection of web pages
choices.explanation: This is reversed — the Internet is the network infrastructure (including hardware); the Web is the collection of web pages and the HTTP protocol that serves them.

choices.text: The World Wide Web is accessible without an Internet connection
choices.explanation: The World Wide Web operates over the Internet — accessing web pages requires an Internet connection.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A research project requires processing 1 terabyte of climate data to find patterns. The computation is too large for a single computer to handle in a reasonable time. Which computing model would best address this limitation?

choices.text: Distributed computing — splitting the data across many computers
choices.explanation: Distributed computing divides tasks across multiple machines — it enables solving problems too large for one computer by sharing both storage and processing.

choices.text: Sequential computing — running all calculations on one powerful computer
choices.explanation: Sequential computing on one machine is exactly the problem — 1 TB of data processing would take too long without parallelization.

choices.text: Increasing bandwidth — making the Internet connection faster
choices.explanation: Bandwidth affects data transfer speed, not processing speed — the bottleneck is computation, not network speed.

choices.text: Using lossless compression to reduce the data to a manageable size
choices.explanation: Compression reduces storage size, not processing complexity — the fundamental problem is computational workload, not file size.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: You send an email with a large attachment. The email is broken into many pieces, sent across different network paths, and reassembled at the destination. Which technology does this describe?

choices.text: Packet switching
choices.explanation: Packet switching breaks data into packets that travel independently across available network paths and are reassembled at the destination — exactly what happens with email transmission.

choices.text: Circuit switching
choices.explanation: Circuit switching establishes a dedicated end-to-end connection before transmitting — the Internet uses packet switching, not circuit switching.

choices.text: Data compression
choices.explanation: Compression reduces file size — breaking data into independently routed packets is packet switching, not compression.

choices.text: Distributed computing
choices.explanation: Distributed computing processes tasks across multiple machines — routing an email as packets is a network transmission concept, not distributed computing.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains why parallel computing does not always produce a speedup equal to the number of processors used?

choices.text: Some parts of a task must be done sequentially and cannot be parallelized, limiting the overall speedup
choices.explanation: Tasks with sequential dependencies cannot benefit from parallelization — the sequential portion becomes a bottleneck that limits maximum speedup regardless of processor count.

choices.text: Parallel computing always runs slower than sequential computing
choices.explanation: Parallel computing often provides significant speedup — the point is that the speedup rarely equals the number of processors due to sequential portions.

choices.text: Adding more processors always doubles the speedup
choices.explanation: Doubling processors rarely doubles speedup — sequential portions and communication overhead limit gains.

choices.text: Parallel computers do not use protocols, so they cannot communicate
choices.explanation: Parallel and distributed systems use protocols for coordination — communication overhead is actually one reason speedup is less than the number of processors.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: You type 'google.com' in a browser and the page loads. Which component translates this human-readable name to the IP address your computer needs?

choices.text: DNS (Domain Name System)
choices.explanation: DNS translates domain names like 'google.com' into numerical IP addresses — your computer queries DNS before it can connect to the web server.

choices.text: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)
choices.explanation: TCP manages reliable packet delivery — it works after the IP address is known, not to translate domain names.

choices.text: HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol)
choices.explanation: HTTP handles web page requests and responses — it operates after DNS has resolved the IP address.

choices.text: A router
choices.explanation: Routers forward packets between networks using IP addresses — they do not translate domain names to IP addresses.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A social media platform grows from 1 million to 100 million users without changing its infrastructure. Users experience very slow performance. Which property is the platform lacking?

choices.text: Scalability
choices.explanation: Scalability is the ability to handle increasing workload — a platform that degrades under higher user load lacks scalability.

choices.text: Fault tolerance
choices.explanation: Fault tolerance means continuing to operate when components fail — the issue here is performance under load, not equipment failure.

choices.text: Redundancy
choices.explanation: Redundancy provides backup paths for fault tolerance — it does not directly address performance under increased user load.

choices.text: Bandwidth
choices.explanation: While bandwidth may be one factor, scalability describes the broader ability to handle growth — the system needs a scalable architecture, not just more bandwidth.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains why a user visiting the same website on two different days might have their data travel along different physical paths?

choices.text: The Internet uses packet switching, where routers dynamically choose the best available path for each packet
choices.explanation: Packet switching routes packets based on current network conditions — a path that was optimal yesterday may be congested today, so routers choose different routes.

choices.text: Each website has a fixed path that never changes
choices.explanation: The Internet uses dynamic routing — paths are chosen based on current conditions, not fixed in advance.

choices.text: The user's IP address changes every day, requiring a new route
choices.explanation: IP addresses can change, but routing decisions are made by routers based on network conditions, not just the user's IP.

choices.text: DNS assigns different IP addresses on different days
choices.explanation: DNS does translate domain names to IPs, but dynamic routing is driven by current network conditions, not DNS changes.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Which protocol guarantees that all packets in a message arrive at their destination and are correctly reassembled?

choices.text: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)
choices.explanation: TCP provides reliable delivery — it numbers packets, confirms receipt, and retransmits lost packets, ensuring complete and correctly ordered data.

choices.text: HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol)
choices.explanation: HTTP is an application-layer protocol for web requests — it relies on TCP for reliable delivery but does not itself handle packet reliability.

choices.text: DNS (Domain Name System)
choices.explanation: DNS translates domain names to IP addresses — it does not handle packet delivery or reassembly.

choices.text: IP (Internet Protocol)
choices.explanation: IP handles addressing and routing but does not guarantee delivery — TCP adds reliability on top of IP.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A data center adds backup power supplies, duplicate servers, and multiple network connections to ensure continued operation during failures. What concept does this demonstrate?

choices.text: Fault tolerance through redundancy
choices.explanation: Redundant components (backup power, duplicate servers, multiple connections) ensure the system keeps working even when individual components fail — this is fault tolerance achieved through redundancy.

choices.text: Scalability through load balancing
choices.explanation: Load balancing distributes traffic to handle more users — duplicating components for failure protection is redundancy for fault tolerance, not scalability.

choices.text: Distributed computing across multiple sites
choices.explanation: Distributed computing splits tasks across locations for computational power — adding backup components for failure protection is redundancy.

choices.text: Sequential computing with failover procedures
choices.explanation: Sequential computing is a processing model — the redundant hardware described here achieves fault tolerance through redundancy.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Three independent tasks each take 10 seconds when run sequentially (30 seconds total). If run in parallel on 3 processors, how long do they take?

choices.text: 10 seconds — all three run simultaneously
choices.explanation: When three independent tasks run simultaneously on three processors, total time equals the longest single task — all three finish in 10 seconds.

choices.text: 30 seconds — parallelism does not reduce time for independent tasks
choices.explanation: Parallelism does reduce time when tasks are independent — three independent 10-second tasks run simultaneously complete in 10 seconds, not 30.

choices.text: 3 seconds — time is divided by the number of processors
choices.explanation: Each task still takes 10 seconds — parallelism runs them concurrently, not 3x faster per task.

choices.text: 20 seconds — one task must wait for another to finish
choices.explanation: If tasks are independent (no dependencies), they can run concurrently — no waiting is required, so all finish in 10 seconds.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A network engineer is designing a critical hospital network. Which design choice would best ensure the network remains operational if any single connection fails?

choices.text: Adding multiple redundant connections between network nodes so data can reroute if one fails
choices.explanation: Redundancy creates multiple paths — if one connection fails, data reroutes through another, maintaining fault tolerance critical for hospital operations.

choices.text: Increasing the bandwidth of the single connection to handle more data
choices.explanation: Higher bandwidth does not prevent failure — a single high-bandwidth connection still creates a single point of failure.

choices.text: Using HTTP instead of TCP for all communication
choices.explanation: HTTP and TCP are communication protocols — they don't prevent physical connection failures. Redundant paths address physical link failures.

choices.text: Assigning each device a fixed IP address that never changes
choices.explanation: Fixed IP addresses help with device identification — they do not prevent connections from failing or provide alternative routing.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Which of the following correctly distinguishes parallel computing from distributed computing?

choices.text: Parallel computing typically uses multiple processors in the same machine; distributed computing uses multiple separate computers
choices.explanation: Parallel computing runs simultaneous processes on multiple processors (often in one machine). Distributed computing splits work across separate networked computers, enabling larger-scale problems.

choices.text: Parallel computing is always faster than distributed computing
choices.explanation: Speed depends on the problem and implementation — distributed computing can be faster for massive-scale problems that don't fit on one machine.

choices.text: Distributed computing cannot be used on the Internet
choices.explanation: The Internet enables distributed computing — cloud computing and large-scale data processing are examples of distributed computing over the Internet.

choices.text: Parallel computing requires at least 100 processors
choices.explanation: Parallel computing can use as few as 2 processors — there is no minimum processor count required.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A task requires Step A to complete before Step B can begin, and Step B to complete before Step C can begin. The steps take 5, 8, and 3 seconds respectively. What is the minimum time to complete the task using any number of processors?

choices.text: 16 seconds — sequential dependencies prevent parallelization
choices.explanation: When steps have strict sequential dependencies (each must complete before the next starts), no amount of parallelization can reduce the total time below 5+8+3=16 seconds.

choices.text: 8 seconds — only the longest step matters in parallel computing
choices.explanation: Only independent tasks can run simultaneously — these steps are sequential dependencies, so all must run in order.

choices.text: 3 seconds — spreading work across more processors reduces each step's time
choices.explanation: Adding more processors does not help when tasks have strict dependencies — Step A must finish before B starts regardless of processor count.

choices.text: 0 seconds — sufficiently powerful computers eliminate all processing time
choices.explanation: Any computation takes some time — even with infinite processors, sequential dependencies require each step to complete before the next begins.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: When you send an email, it may travel through dozens of different routers before reaching the recipient. Each router decides where to forward the email next based on network conditions. What does each router use to make this decision?

choices.text: The destination IP address in the packet
choices.explanation: Routers examine the destination IP address in each packet to determine where to forward it — routing tables map destinations to the best available next hop.

choices.text: The sender's email username
choices.explanation: Routers work at the network layer using IP addresses — email usernames are application-layer data that routers do not inspect.

choices.text: The subject line of the email
choices.explanation: Routers forward packets based on IP addresses, not email content — the subject line is application-layer data.

choices.text: The DNS name of the sending server
choices.explanation: Routers use IP addresses for forwarding decisions — DNS names are resolved to IP addresses before packets are sent.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A network diagram shows that any two locations can communicate via at least 3 different paths. If a storm damages one path, communication continues. Which statement best describes this network?

choices.text: The network is fault tolerant because redundant paths allow rerouting when one fails
choices.explanation: Multiple paths provide redundancy — when one fails, data reroutes through the remaining paths, maintaining communication. This is fault tolerance.

choices.text: The network is scalable because it has many users
choices.explanation: Scalability refers to handling increased workload — multiple paths for fault tolerance address failure resilience, not user capacity.

choices.text: The network uses distributed computing to process data
choices.explanation: Distributed computing processes tasks across machines — redundant network paths are an infrastructure design feature for fault tolerance.

choices.text: The network has high bandwidth because there are multiple connections
choices.explanation: More paths can increase total bandwidth but the key design feature here is fault tolerance through redundancy — surviving the loss of any single path.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: An online store must handle 100 customer orders per second during normal hours, but during a holiday sale it handles 10,000 orders per second without slowing down. Which property does the store's system demonstrate?

choices.text: Scalability
choices.explanation: Scalability is the ability to handle increasing workload — managing a 100x increase in orders without degradation demonstrates excellent scalability.

choices.text: Fault tolerance
choices.explanation: Fault tolerance maintains operation when components fail — the system here handles more load, not component failures.

choices.text: Distributed computing
choices.explanation: While distributed computing might be how scalability is achieved, the property being demonstrated is scalability — the ability to handle increased load.

choices.text: Redundancy
choices.explanation: Redundancy is duplicate components for fault tolerance — handling more transactions is scalability, not redundancy.

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains what TCP does when a packet is lost during transmission?

choices.text: TCP detects the missing packet and requests the sender to retransmit it
choices.explanation: TCP numbers packets and requires acknowledgment — if a packet isn't confirmed, TCP requests retransmission, ensuring complete and correct delivery.

choices.text: The message is discarded and the sender must send the entire message again
choices.explanation: TCP only retransmits the missing packet(s), not the entire message — this efficiency is a key feature of TCP.

choices.text: The receiver reconstructs the missing packet from the others
choices.explanation: TCP requests retransmission of missing packets — it does not reconstruct missing data from existing packets.

choices.text: TCP uses redundant paths to send every packet simultaneously
choices.explanation: TCP does not send duplicate packets by default — it retransmits only when a packet is confirmed lost.
